For Africans living between two worlds

The truth your family won't tell you — told with love.

This is Tendai — a writer and qualified mental-health practitioner of African heritage. For more than two decades, Tendai has worked closely with people of the diaspora and seen what no one warns you about: the money guilt, the identity ache, raising children between two cultures, the pull of home. This is the honest conversation — and a clear way forward.

What we talk about here

The things the community whispers, said out loud

No shame. No sugar-coating. Every hard truth comes with a tool you can use today.

— On money

Your family can't see your bills

They see your address abroad, not your rent. Learn to give without bleeding — boundaries that keep love intact.

— On identity

Too foreign for home, too African for here

The identity ache no one prepared you for, and the path to claiming both worlds without apology.

— On your children

They're becoming people you don't recognise

That's partly the point. How to raise confident two-world children without losing them or yourself.

Why listen to Tendai

A working life spent helping people through the realities of diaspora life.

Of African heritage, Tendai has lived in the UK and Australia and spent a working lifetime among the diaspora. Decades of close work with people living between worlds have shown Tendai what the journey asks of them — and why the usual advice so rarely fits.

As a qualified mental-health practitioner of African heritage with more than two decades in the field, trained and practised in the UK, Tendai has worked alongside many people of the diaspora and seen, first-hand, the particular challenges they carry. So this isn’t guesswork, and it isn’t any one person’s private story. It is professional understanding drawn from many lives, written for everyone — which is why this platform speaks to your actual life, not a Western template.

Read Tendai’s story
The Between Worlds Guides

Seventeen honest books. One for every part of the diaspora life.

Each one tells a hard truth — and hands you a clear guide to success. Beginning with Book One.

See the full series
New · for couples
The newest honest book

Who Wears the Trousers?

When the marriage you were raised to expect meets the diaspora — she’s a professional too, sometimes earning more; the load at home is unfair; the bed grows cold — the old roles collide, and too many good marriages quietly break.

This is a book for couples to read together. Even-handed and compassionate to both husband and wife, it names the hard truths plainly, then turns every time towards repair — and a way back to partnership.

Read about it Tell me when it launches
For every age between worlds

Whatever your age, there’s a place for you here.

Living between worlds doesn’t start in adulthood. It begins in childhood and shapes a whole life. Between Worlds now speaks to every stage — so your children, your young adults, and you are all spoken to honestly.

☀️
Children · 7–14
You Belong Here
For a child who feels like the only one who looks like them. Warm, gentle, and reassuring — a book that helps them feel proud, safe, and certain they belong.
Free guide
🌟
Young adults · 17–25
Not Your Experiment
For when the West is dazzled by you for the wrong reasons. How to tell who truly values you, keep your worth, and keep yourself — empowering, never frightening.

The twelve-book series, plus standalone books — one honest guide for every part of the diaspora life: money, family, identity, parenting, faith, racism, the pull of home.
A free gift for the youngest among us
Free · ages 7–14

If a child you love feels like the only one…

You Belong Here is a warm, gentle book for the child who is the only one who looks like them — in their class, their town, their world. It helps them feel proud of where they come from, comfortable being different, and certain that they belong.

It’s free, because every child who feels alone in a room deserves to hear this. Read it with them, and let them know: you belong here.

The Diaspora Money Boundaries Toolkit

The exact words to say no to family requests — without guilt, without breaking the bond. Scripts, a simple budget frame, and how to give from strength, not pressure. My gift to you for joining the list.

My Story

Tendai doesn’t write about the diaspora from a distance.

A writer of African heritage and a qualified mental-health practitioner with more than two decades in the field. Everything Tendai shares is grounded in close, long-term work alongside the people who live these realities.

The name Tendai means, in Shona, give thanks — a fitting name, because for so many in the diaspora, gratitude and difficulty walk side by side. There is much to be thankful for in the lives people build abroad. There is also a cost rarely spoken about out loud. Between Worlds is where Tendai speaks about it honestly.

Tendai has lived in the UK and Australia and built a career in mental health, spending many years close to the diaspora experience. There is a loneliness that can live underneath the opportunity, and a quiet weight of expectation that follows people across an ocean — truths that became unmistakable through years of listening to, and working with, those who carry them.

What that long closeness makes clear is that “between worlds” is never really about one country left behind or another arrived in. It is a way of living — holding more than one world at once, fully at home in none of them and in all of them. It is the thread running through every life these books are written for.

“Surviving was the entry fee. It was never meant to be the whole life.”

Over many years of close work, Tendai has watched, up close, what living between worlds asks of people: the workplaces where people are made to feel small in ways too quiet to name; the families back home who see an address abroad and imagine a wealth that isn’t there; the strain that distance, money, and changing roles place on marriages and on the next generation. Tendai writes about these things because they have been seen, again and again — not in one home, but across a whole community.

For a long time, many in the diaspora believe they are the only ones. Then they begin to talk — really talk — to other Africans living the same life, and the same story comes back, again and again, in different accents. The same wounds. The same silence. The same hunger for someone to simply tell the truth. That is the conversation these books come from.

There is something else worth knowing, because it shapes how these books are written. Tendai is a qualified mental-health practitioner with more than two decades in the field, who has lived in the UK and Australia. So when Tendai writes about the weight people carry — the anxiety, the grief, the silence, the strain on families — it isn’t theory, and it isn’t guesswork. These are struggles witnessed first-hand, day after day, in professional practice — the particular ways they show up in the lives of people across the diaspora. That close, careful understanding sits underneath every page.

One thing Tendai is always clear about: the stories and examples in these books are not portraits of any one family, least of all the author’s own. They are drawn from more than two decades of listening, observing, and working alongside many, many people across the diaspora — woven together so that no single person, household, or family is ever on the page. If readers recognise themselves in them, it is because these experiences are so widely shared, not because they belong to anyone in particular. Tendai writes about everyone, with care for all, and with judgement towards none.

"I'm not here to judge you. I'm the uncle who tells you what your friends won't — because I love you."

That is what Between Worlds is. Not motivation. Not pretending everything is fine. Honest conversation about the real issues of diaspora life — money, family, identity, parenting, faith, racism, the pull of home — paired with a clear, researched path forward. Because naming the pain is only half the work. There also has to be a way through.

Welcome to the table. Pull up a chair.

A note on the stories in these books

The people, families, marriages, and situations described in Tendai’s books are not portraits of any real person — and they are certainly not portraits of the author’s own family. They are composites and illustrations, woven together from a great many lives observed and listened to over more than two decades, so that no single person or household is ever depicted.

If you recognise yourself, or someone you love, in these pages, it is not because a book is about you. It is because these experiences are so widely shared across the diaspora that they belong, in a sense, to all of us. Tendai writes about everyone, with deep care for all, and with judgement towards none.

The Between Worlds Guides

Sixteen books for the life between two worlds.

A twelve-book guide to diaspora life, two standalone books, and a youth wave for the next generation. Each tells a truth no one else will — and offers a clear way through.

For children (7–14), young adults (17–25), and adults — every age between worlds.

The Between Worlds Guides

The twelve-book series at the heart of it all — one honest guide for each part of diaspora life.

Three more honest books

Standalone books that tell harder truths — one that turns to speak to the people who love us, and one for couples to read together.

The You Belong Here Youth Wave

Carrying the message to the next generation — from childhood to young adulthood. You are a whole person, of real worth, and you belong.

Be first to know when the books launch.

Letters from Between Worlds

Honest letters from the diaspora — now and then.

Short, honest essays on the real things we carry — written when there’s something worth saying, not on a schedule. No noise, no filler. Just a letter, now and then, from one of us to another.

Beginning soon

The first letters are on their way.

This is where I’ll write to you directly — not a blog, not content for the sake of it, but the kind of honest letter I wish someone had written me along the way. On the things we don’t say out loud. On the small wins and the quiet griefs. On what these two (and now three) worlds keep teaching me.

There’s no fixed schedule — a letter arrives when there’s something real to say. Join the list, and you’ll get each one the day it’s written.

  • —“The phone call I almost didn’t answer.”  On guilt, family, and the cost of always being the strong one.
  • —“Belonging nowhere, and everywhere.”  On the strange gift of being at home in more than one world at once.
  • —“What I’d tell my younger self at the airport.”  A letter to everyone just arriving.

A few of the letters I’m working on. They’ll land here — and in your inbox, if you’d like.

No noise. A letter only when there’s something real to say — and the free Money Boundaries Toolkit when you join. Your email stays private, always.

Speaking & Workshops

Bring the honest conversation to your community.

For diaspora organisations, churches and mosques, universities, and companies serious about belonging. Drawing on more than two decades as a qualified mental-health practitioner of African heritage, working closely with people of the diaspora and seeing these issues first-hand.

What I speak on

Talks that name the real thing

🪙

The Money Between Us

Black tax, remittance guilt, and giving from strength — for community groups and families.

🌍

Identity Without Apology

Belonging between two worlds — for student societies and young professionals.

🧡

Healing the Diaspora Wound

Mental health and the cost of "you should be grateful" — for faith and wellbeing groups.

🏢

Belonging at Work

Microaggressions, inclusion and dignity — for corporate D&I and leadership teams.
